I feel the weave of the world as the very grain of the wood I run my hands over
Mapping the neighborhood with my marrow, knowing exactly which floorboard groans under the weight of a secret
The dough under my hands is cool, then warm, then alive—a conversation requiring no translation
The hum of the world feels like the vibration of a refrigerator in a quiet kitchen
The pale, fine dust of flour on my hands—distributed, necessary, and unnoticed
The heavy, sudden intensity of the tethered ones as they inhabit their bodies before vanishing from the corner
The scent of damp wool and the sharp, metallic tang of the morning air trailing behind her
